entry.title: Planning to Deploy SAP BW/4HANA 2023? Understand the Limitations and Possibilities for SAP BW 7.5.
entry.content:
Hi,

Greetings!

Are you planning for a Conversion to SAPBW/4HANA 2023, from SAP BW 7.5, please check out few notes to consider:

Pre-requisite for SAP BW7.50 version:

 

Incase already on SAP BW/4HANA 1.0, follow the simple and smooth 2 step Upgrade:

Step-1) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023
Step-2) SAP BW/4HANA 1.0 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2021 -> SAP BW/4HANA 2023

Please note - Currently direct Upgrade from SAP BW 1.0 to SAP BW/4HANA 2023 is not possible!!!

Incase if you are planning any of the existing Conversion approaches based upon the existing SAP BW Version(SAP BW 7.x to BW4HANA 2021 or BW4HANA 2023), whether it is REMOTE or SHELL or In-Place, a few findings from my experience are listed for your reference before starting the actual Realization Phase of any Conversion:

1)Consider the Discovery Phase as knowledge platform, work closely with SAP-BASIS to implement Maintenance Planner.

2)Note down the findings of Readiness Check and Simplification List.

3)Align with the Upcoming /Planned Source-System Upgrades/Migrations.

4)Define the scope of Business Applications and carve out the unwanted flows.

5)Cleanup the Obsolete Source Systems along with the dependent Objects which are persistent in the system

6)Create a Production Copy of BW System and try to simulate the Conversion which gives more insights on the functionalities which are replaced with new Simplified Info Providers, Data Extraction Platform (ODP), Reporting Tools, Modelling Tools et.,

7)Perform data Consistency checks by leveraging the SAP Delivered Programs to handle the repairing of Master Data and SIDs, note down Listed Objects, this will help to analyze and get well-prepared for handling the actual data migration in Production Landscape.

8)Do the ABAP Code Compatibility Checks which can be initiated from Conversion Cockpit. This can be done multiple times to ensure the Sender System is ready for Conversion.

9)Go for periodic system Backups at various phases of Conversion.

10)List out the incompatible Extractors and enable the marker for ODP Extraction.

Consider the findings from Discovery Phase and carry forward the fixes, finding and alternative workarounds based upon the customization factor of data models and code, then will help to succeed the Transformation and plan the downtime of Production during Go-Live.

HAPPY Conversion! Get ready to enable Real-time Data Processing, Simplified modeling and Administration with prefabricated templates.

 

Many Thanks,

Sunil Babu Sanjamala

entry.title: How to upload Excel Spreadsheet on SAP Fiori UI5 Freestyle
entry.content:
Introduction

The Fiori UI5 demo kit works perfectly for most part of the examples. For this one particularly, my complain is that it does not import the file on the frontend, but the File Uploader calls the backend instead (or maybe I missed something)

From my perspective and my scenario, I needed something "simple" that could get all the spreadsheet data and call a service using JSON

Creating the popup to upload the file

To have a cleaner code, I decided to create the upload popup using  fragment

Create the button on your view to call the fragment

 

<Button text="Upload" press="onOpenUploadSheet"/>

 

Create the fragment like this one - please, remove what is not needed for your example.
Important to mention that if the property "sameFilenameAllowed" is false you will not be able to select the file, click on cancel, and click on the same file again (I thought it was a bug until find this property)

 

<c:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout"
    xmlns:u="sap.ui.unified"
    xmlns:c="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns:up="sap.m.upload">
    <Dialog id="UploadSheetDialog" title="{i18n>UploadSheet}" busyIndicatorDelay="0">
        <u:FileUploader id="fileUploader" class="sapUiMediumMargin" tooltip="{18n>UploadSheetTooltip}" fileType="xlsx"
			placeholder="{i18n>UploadSheetPlaceholder}" typeMissmatch="onUploadSheeetTypeMismatch"
            change="onUploadSheetChange" sameFilenameAllowed="true" width="70%"/>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="{i18n>upload}" press="onConfirmUploadSheet" type="Emphasized"/>
        </beginButton>
        <endButton>
            <Button text="{i18n>cancel}" press="onCancelUploadSheet" type="Reject"/>
        </endButton>
    </Dialog>
</c:FragmentDefinition>

 

Creating the Excel upload logic

On the view controller, create the following logic. The function onOpenUploadSheet is called by the button created on the view.xml.
If the user click on cancel, it will call onCancelUploadSheet

 

onOpenUploadSheet: function () {
      this._displayUploadSheet();
},

_displayUploadSheet: function () {
      this._countSheet = null;
      if (!this._oUploadSheetDialog) {
        Fragment.load({
          id: this.getView().getId(),
          name: "<your>.<project>.view.UploadSheet",
          controller: this,
        }).then(
          function (dialog) {
            this._oUploadSheetDialog = dialog;
            this.getView().addDependent(this._oUploadSheetDialog);
            this._oUploadSheetDialog.open();
          }.bind(this)
        );
      } else {
        this.byId("fileUploader").setValue(null).clear();
        this._oUploadSheetDialog.open();
      }
    },

onCancelUploadSheet: function () {
      this._countSheet = null;
      if (this._oUploadSheetDialog) {
        this._oUploadSheetDialog.close();
      }
},

 

A difference compared to https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F1S0LOAkTak is that we cannot use getFocusDomRef() anymore (check this discussion https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/fileuploader-sapui5-version-1-103/qaq-p/12598852). To overcome this difference, we can use the event change, so I created the function "onUploadSheetChange".
I am simply storing the file uploaded into a global variable

 

onUploadSheetChange: function (oEvent) {
      this._countSheet = {
        file: oEvent.getParameter("files")[0],
        XLSXImport: true,
      };
},

 

When the user click on Confirm, it will call "onConfirmUploadSheet". This function checks if the global variable _countSheet is not null to proceed - please, import MessageBox if you want to use this control for error handling. The internal method _getText works to facilitate i18n texts

Once the file upload is completed, it will call the function "_onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted", which uses an external library to import the Excel spreadsheet

 

    onConfirmUploadSheet: function (oEvent) {
      if (!this._countSheet) {
        MessageBox.error(this._getText("noFileSelected"));
        return;
      }

      this.byId("UploadSheetDialog").setBusy(true);

      const reader = new FileReader();
      reader.onload = this._onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted.bind(this);
      reader.readAsBinaryString(this._countSheet.file);
    },

    _onUploadSheetXSLXCompleted: function (file) {
      try {
        const workbook = XLSX.read(file, {
          type: "binary",
        });

        const sheet = XLSX.utils.sheet_to_row_object_array(workbook.Sheets["Upload"]);
        
      } catch (error) {
        MessageBox.error(this._getText("xlsxCannotBeRead"));
        return [];
      }
    },

    _getText: function (sKey, args) {
      return this.getOwnerComponent().getModel("i18n").getResourceBundle().getText(sKey, args);
    },

 

The last step is to import the external library to Fiori. To do so, you can download the code from cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/xlsx/0.18.0/xlsx.js
Create a new folder called libs and create a file called xlsx.js with the code above

Open manifest.json and add a new resource on "sap.ui5"."resources" section 

 

"resources": {
      "js": [
        {
          "uri": "libs/xlsx.js"
        }
      ]
    },

entry.title: SAP ABAP: Explore Data Type Conversions with 30 Examples
entry.content:
Introduction

Data type conversions are fundamental in any programming language, and SAP ABAP is no exception. Whether you're converting strings to integers, handling dates, or managing packed numbers, understanding these conversions can enhance the robustness and accuracy of your ABAP applications. In this blog post, I'll show 30 examples of data type conversions in ABAP—some might seem obvious, but they often come with unexpected challenges and exceptions.

Data Type Conversions in ABAP

Below, I'll walk you through a series of data type conversions using a simple ABAP program. The program demonstrates different scenarios, including handling exceptions that arise during conversions.

1. Normal String

REPORT ztest_sk. 
DATA: lv_int     TYPE i,
      lv_date    TYPE d,
      lv_time    TYPE t,
      lv_numc(3) TYPE n,
      lv_str     TYPE string,
      lv_char    TYPE c LENGTH 3,
      lv_pack    TYPE p LENGTH 5 DECIMALS 2,
      lv_utc     TYPE utclong. 

lv_str = 'ABCD12.3'.
WRITE /: '01. String: '. WRITE lv_str.

2. String to Character

lv_char = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '02. String to Character3: '. WRITE lv_char.

Note: Data loss can occur when converting strings to smaller character fields, as shown in this example.

3. Exception: String to Character with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_char = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_char.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_data_loss.  
    WRITE /: '03. String to Character3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_DATA_LOSS exception'.  
ENDTRY.

Insight: Using EXACT #() with string to character conversion can result in exceptions if data loss is detected.

4. Alphanumeric String to Integer

TRY.  
    lv_int = lv_str.  
    WRITE /: lv_int.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '04. String to integer: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

Challenge: Converting non-numeric strings to integers triggers exception.

5. String to Date

lv_date = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '05. String to date: '. WRITE lv_date.

6. Exception: String to Date with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_date = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_date.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date.  
    WRITE /: '06. String to date w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception'.  
ENDTRY.

7. String to Time

lv_time = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '07. String to time: '. WRITE lv_time.

8. Exception: String to Time with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_time = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_time.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_time.  
    WRITE /: '08. String to time w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_TIME exception'.  
ENDTRY.

9. String to Number

lv_numc = lv_str.  
WRITE /: '09. String to number3: '. WRITE lv_numc.

10. Exception: String to Number with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_numc = EXACT #( lv_str ).  
    WRITE /: lv_numc.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '10. String to number3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

11. Exception: String to Packed Number

TRY.  
    lv_pack = lv_str.  
    WRITE /: lv_pack.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_number.  
    WRITE /: '11. String to Packed5_2: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception'.  
ENDTRY.

12. Packed Number Handling

lv_pack =  '33.12456'.  
WRITE /: '12. Packed5_2 (33.12456): '. WRITE lv_pack.

13. Exception: Packed Number with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_pack = EXACT #( 1 / 8 ).  
    WRITE /: lv_pack.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_rounding.  
    WRITE /: '13. Packed5_2 (1/8) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_ROUNDING exception'.  
ENDTRY.

14. Date Handling

DATA lv_date1 TYPE sy-datum.  
lv_date1 =  '20241233'.  
WRITE /: '14. Date (20241233): '. WRITE lv_date1 USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____'.

15. Exceptions: Date with EXACT #()

TRY.  
    lv_date = EXACT #( '202401233' ).  
    WRITE /: lv_date.  
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date.  
    WRITE /: '15. Date (20241233) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception'.  
ENDTRY.

16. Date to Integer

lv_int =  sy-datum.  
WRITE /: '16. Date to Integer (sy-datum) = Days from 01.01.0001): '.  
WRITE: sy-datum USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____', ' --> ', lv_int.

17. Integer to Date

lv_int = lv_int + 1.  
lv_date1 =  lv_int.  
WRITE /: '17. Integer to Date (sy-datum + 1) = Days from 01.01.0001): '.  
WRITE: lv_int, ' --> ', lv_date1 USING EDIT MASK '__/__/____'.

18. Time to Integer

lv_int =  sy-uzeit.  
WRITE /: '18. Time to Integer (sy-uzeit) = Seconds from 00:00:00): '.  
WRITE: sy-uzeit USING EDIT MASK '__:__:__', ' --> ', lv_int.

19. Integer to Time

DATA lv_time1 TYPE sy-uzeit.  
lv_int = lv_int + 3600.  
lv_time1 =  lv_int.  
WRITE /: '19. Integer to Time (sy-uzeit + 3600 sec) = Seconds from 00:00:00): '.  
WRITE: lv_int, ' --> ', lv_time1 USING EDIT MASK '__:__:__'.

20. Direct Date Difference

lv_date = '20240101'.  
lv_int = sy-datum - lv_date.  
WRITE /: '20. Date Difference (sy-datum) - ''20240101''): '. WRITE lv_int.

21. Character from Packed

lv_char = '1.23'.  
WRITE /: '21. Packed(1.23) to Character3: '. WRITE lv_char.

 

UTC Long Handling

Converting various data types to UTC long often results in exceptions since these formats might not be compatible.

22. Alphanumeric String to UTClong

TRY.
    lv_utc = lv_str.
    WRITE /: '22. String to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '22. String to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

23. Character3 to UTClong

lv_char = lv_str.
TRY.
    lv_utc = lv_char.
    WRITE /: '23. Character3 to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '23. Character3 to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

24. Numeric String to UTClong

TRY.
    lv_str = '20241220'.
    lv_utc = lv_str.
    WRITE /: '24. String (20241220) to UTC long: '. WRITE lv_utc.
  CATCH cx_sy_conversion_no_date_time.
    WRITE /: '24. String (20241220) to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception'. WRITE lv_utc.
ENDTRY.

25-29. Impossible UTC conversions

WRITE /: '25. Date can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '26. Time can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '27. Number can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '28. Integer can''t be converted to UTC long'.
WRITE /: '29. Packed can''t be converted to UTC long'.

30. System UTClong.

lv_utc = utclong_current( ).  
WRITE /: '30. System UTC time: '. WRITE lv_utc.

Conclusion

Navigating through data type conversions in ABAP can be a complex task that requires careful handling of exceptions and understanding of underlying data structures. By exploring these 30 examples, you gain a clearer perspective on how conversions impact data integrity and application logic.

Feel free to experiment with these examples in your own systems and unlock new insights into the world of ABAP programming.

Program Output:

01. String: ABCD12.3
02. String to Character3: ABC
03. String to Character3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_DATA_LOSS exception
04. String to integer: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
05. String to date: .312ABCD
06. String to date w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception
07. String to time: ABCD12
08. String to time w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_TIME exception
09. String to number3: 123
10. String to number3 w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
11. String to Packed5_2: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_NUMBER exception
12. Packed5_2 (33.12456): 33,12
13. Packed5_2 (1/8) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_ROUNDING exception
14. Date (20241233): 33/12/2024
15. Date (20241233) w/ EXACT#: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE exception
16. Date to Integer (sy-datum) = Days from 01.01.0001): 20/12/2024 > 739.241
17. Integer to Date (sy-datum + 1) = Days from 01.01.0001): 739.242 > 21/12/2024
18. Time to Integer (sy-uzeit) = Seconds from 00:00:00): 11:50:51 > 42.651
19. Integer to Time (sy-uzeit + 3600 sec) = Seconds from 00:00:00): 46.251 > 12:50:51
20. Date Difference (sy-datum) - '20240101'): 354
21. Packed(1.23) to Character3: 1.2
22. String to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
23. String (20241220) to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
24. Character3 to UTC long: CX_SY_CONVERSION_NO_DATE_TIME exception
25. Date can't be converted to UTC long
26. Time can't be converted to UTC long
27. Number can't be converted to UTC long
28. Integer can't be converted to UTC long
29. Packed can't be converted to UTC long
30. System UTC time: 20.12.2024 06:20:51,5742130

Happy Coding!

entry.title: Managing Event Volume: Summary Events and Grouping Events in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
The proactive resolution of integration issues is crucial for maintaining business continuity in a distributed yet interconnected customer landscape. Customers can define events in Integration and Exception Monitoring in  SAP Cloud ALM to trigger notifications or alerts whenever integration issues with specific statuses, such as "Error," are detected.

For instance, an event can be created for every error message, each of which corresponds to a business transaction that is impacted. However, it is essential to maintain a manageable number of events. When the volume of events becomes too high, it becomes challenging to prioritize and address them efficiently, which can delay issue resolution.

In cases of systemic issues, there may be a surge in error messages, leading to an event flooding situation that requires effective management and affect resource utilization. Using summary events and grouping of events within Integration and Exception Monitoring, customers can help control such event flooding, ensuring smoother and more efficient event management.

Refer the blog for more information on various events configuration parameters for the event ‘Issues Detected’ in Integration and Exception Monitoring in SAP Cloud ALM.

Summary Event

Customer Need:

Suppose the customer wishes to set up an event under the following conditions:

  •  All messages with a status of FAILED, ESCALATED, or PROCESSING.
  •  Messages that remain in the specified status for more than 15 minutes.
  •  An event should be generated for every individual message that meets these conditions.
  • No more than 10 events should be generated during each calculation period (5 minutes).
  • Email notifications should be sent to specific users.

Customer Configuration:

To fulfil the above requirement, the customer can set up the metric "Issues Detected" with the following configuration:

  • In the Filter section:
    • Remove the prefilled values for the status group and add the required values for status: FAILED.
    • Note: "Status" refers to the status of messages in the local cloud service. The status group maps these statuses to a specific predefined status group in SAP Cloud ALM. For example, the FAILED status in Cloud Integration is mapped to the Status Group "Error" in SAP Cloud ALM.
  • In the Parameter section:
    •  Set "Messages older than n minutes" to 15 minutes.
    •  Set "Number of events per calculation" to 10.
  • In the Grouping section:
    • Configure the parameters to include "Artifact Name", "Status", "Status Text", and "Message ID".
  • In the Event Action section:
    • Enable the email notification option and add the recipient emails.

How the configuration unfolds:

Now let’s take an example where we have 12 messages with a FAILED status in one monitoring data collection.

  • According to the configuration, the application will search for all messages that have been in the FAILED status for more than 15 minutes. 12 messages are detected.
  • The application will then group these messages as per the configuration. Since the grouping includes the Message ID as a parameter, and each message has a unique Message ID, we will end up with 12 distinct events.
  • Given that the number of events (12) is greater than the "number of events per calculation" (10), 1 summary event will be triggered, and all messages will be mapped to this summary event.
  • The user will receive 1 summary email, with 12 message details contributing to the event.
  • Sample email screen shot is given below for reference.

 

Grouping of Events

Customer Need:

Suppose the customer wishes to set up an event-raising configuration under the following conditions:

  • All messages with a status of FAILED, ESCALATED, or PROCESSING.
  • Messages that remain in the specified status for more than 15 minutes.
  • Events should be grouped by “Artifact Name” and “Status Text” for each relevant message.
  • No more than 10 events should be generated during each calculation period (5 minutes).
  • Email notifications should be sent to specific users.

Customer Configuration:

The customer can keep all the configurations as the previous example. Adjust the configuration in grouping section to reflect the grouping preference.

To fulfil the above requirement, the customer can set up the metric "Issues Detected" with the following configuration:

  • In the Filter section:
    • Remove the prefilled values for the status group and add the required values for status: FAILED.
    • Note: "Status" refers to the status of messages in the local cloud service. The status group maps these statuses to a specific predefined status group in SAP Cloud ALM. For example, the FAILED status in Cloud Integration is mapped to the Status Group "Error" in SAP Cloud ALM.
  • In the Parameter section:
    •  Set "Messages older than n minutes" to 15 minutes.
    •  Set "Number of events per calculation" to 10.
  • In the Grouping section:
    • Configure the parameters to include "Artifact Name", "Status Text".
  • In the Event Action section:
    • Enable the email notification option and add the recipient emails.
    • Create Alerts

How the configuration unfolds:

 

Now let’s take an example where we have 12 messages with a FAILED status in one monitoring data collection. Some messages for “Artifact Name=Replicate Business Partner from SAP Business Suite” and some for “Artifact Name =Confirm Business Partner Replication from SAP Business Suite”.

  • According to the configuration, the application will search for all messages that have been in the FAILED status for more than 15 minutes.12 messages are detected.
  • The application will then group these messages as per the configuration. Since the grouping parameters include "Artifact Name" and "Status Text," and all contributing messages having the same values for these parameters will be grouped.
  • We have 2 groups created.
    • Group 1: “Artifact Name = Replicate Business Partner from SAP Business Suite” and “Status Text= Inbound processing in endpoint at /MDM/C4C/ReplicateBusinessPartner failed with message "Fault:Unsup...”.
    • Group 2: “Artifact Name = Confirm Business Partner Replication from SAP Business Suite” and “Status Text = Inbound processing in endpoint at /MDM/C4C/BusinessPartnerSUITEBulkReplicateConfirmation_Out failed..”
  •  The user will receive 2 emails, one for each group and containing details of the messages contributing to this group. Group 1 will show 4 messages and group 2 will show 8 messages.
  • The user will also receive 2 alerts in Alert Inbox in Integration and Exception monitoring, 1 alert for each group. Alert for group 1 will display 4 messages and alert for group 2 will display 8 messages.

For more information on how to configure these settings, refer to Setting Up Events in SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Stop Guessing! Use Case with Conversational AI Agents in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever stared at your analytics dashboard, wishing it could do more—like answer your questions without having to solely search, filter, and drill into your data? Well, guess what – you can do that now!

In this blog, I’ll walk you through an exciting use case that leverages SAP AI Core with SAP’s AI Agent through the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) in the following order:

  1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View
  2. What are AI Agents?
  3. Why SAP AI Agents?
  4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View
  5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View

Imagine you’re a business user at a Gas Provider Company, exploring your Finance and Billing Dashboards. As you dive into the billing data, a critical insight catches your eye: late fees are unusually high in a region. This sparks concern about potential customer dissatisfaction and retention issues. Here’s where the power of AI shines. By leveraging a Conversational AI Agent directly within your SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard, you can uncover the underlying trends, identify contributing economic factors, gain actionable recommendations, and more – removing the need to “guess” your next steps.

Let me show you how a Gas Provider Company can use a Conversational AI Agent to accelerate decision-making and uncover deeper insights beyond the 4 walls of their Enterprise. Watch the 'Stop Guessing' Use Case Demo below!

As you saw in the video above, the business user was able to receive an output that combines their Enterprise data from the HANA database and External data from PDFs and websites. This blog will further showcase how this capability allows businesses to have insights beyond the data in their 4 walls resulting in realistic, reliable results to drive business decision making. In other words, customers are no longer limited in data and information. With AI Agents you have the possibility to leverage a multitude of tools thereby providing you with a competitive edge. In fact, Gartner has predicted that by 2028 one third of interactions with generative AI will use autonomous AI Agents to complete tasks (Source).

To further understand the value SAP AI Core Services bring with AI Agents, we must first understand what an AI Agent is.

2. What are AI Agents?

AI Agents are NOT chatbots, but rather they are autonomous applications powered by large language models (LLMs) that can integrate with applications and business processes to achieve complex goals. They can fall into two categories:

  1. Task Oriented – Agents can automate workflows and multi-step tasks by leveraging integrations such as with SAP Build Process Automation.
  2. Conversational – Enable natural, human-like conversations to help users gain deeper insights as seen in the use case.

 Agents achieve their goal by using multi-step planning, self-examination and reflection of work to propose improvements, tool usage, and multi-agent collaboration to split-up tasks and come up with a solution. Some of these concepts will be seen in more detail in the ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case -Technical View section.

For example, in the use case video, the Conversational AI Agent examined the prompt in multiple steps by calling on the data and tools. In parallel, it also performed reflection activities to continuously learn and improve. Although there was no multi-agent collaboration here, an example would be a specific scenario AI Agent such as triggering a workflow to automate PR creations for approved marketing promo campaigns or, perhaps, calling on another Conversational AI Agent that has PR information specific tools to achieve goals around answering PR related topics.

 Source: Agents is the most important BTP release this 2024

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 

3. Why SAP AI Agents?

The market for AI Agents is rapidly growing with innovations from OpenAI, Microsoft, Google and other contributors. What makes SAP AI Agents valuable?

  1. SAP AI Agents integrate your semantically rich SAP data and processes, ensuring that the meaning of your business is captured. In other words, SAP knows SAP the best as it can understand, integrate, and capture your data's Business Context (eg. Hierarchies, metadata, currency conversion, languages, security configuration) - providing meaning to the AI Agent to reduce hallucinations and removing the need of manual rebuild of your data’s business context.
  2. The AI Agent integrates with SAP AI Core services by collecting data and leveraging underlying large language model (LLM) within the AI Core. It is also leverages HANA's Vector Engine for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). This advanced setup minimizes hallucinations, delivering more accurate, reliable, and trusted results.
  3. By leveraging SAP AI Core services and other BTP offerings, this will allow for improved efficiency and collaboration across your integrated Intelligent Enterprise Portfolio. You can create enterprise-grade AI Agents tailored to your unique needs that integrate seamlessly with tools like HANA, OData applications, SAP Build Process Automation, and even external sources like websites and PDFs, while ensuring you are the decision maker at the center of it all. This ensures secure interactions with your business data, processes, and systems while adhering to SAP’s clean core principles.

 

4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View

 

In this section, we will dive deeper in the Use Case and explore the use case architecture and witness a Lab Preview of the Project Agent Builder – the new approach to develop agent-based projects in BTP.

Use Case Architecture

In this use case, the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard is leveraging data from the HANA database that has been further transformed and modeled in SAP Datasphere. With SAP Datasphere's native connectivity to SAC, the data maintains it's Business Context and can be consumed in real-time analytics. In addition, the Conversational AI Agent has been embedded into SAC as a custom application widget via Business Application Studio (and can also be embedded via SAP Build Code).

 

 

The AI Agent leverages 3 tools in this use case: Semantically rich data from HANA, Financial PDF documents, and the Gas Providers' Supplier Website. If required more tools can be added beyond the 3, allowing you to have that flexibility to achieve your goal and leverage Enterprise data with External data.

When a question is asked to the Conversational AI Agent in SAC, it sends this to the LLM and grounds it using the HANA Vector Engine & RAG while searching the tools for the appropriate data to respond back with. In addition, it performs muti-step planning to search for information using the 3 assigned tools to produce an answer while continuously reflecting for improvement.

Project Agent Builder

Project Agent Builder is where you can create your Conversational Agent and define the various tools and integrations to achieve your goal. Below you will see a lab preview screenshot of the Project Agent Builder. Upon creating a new Agent, you must identify the following:

  1. Description of the Agent’s expertise and goal with instructions to illustrate the Agent’s behaviour.
  2. Maximum thinking or multi-step planning steps it must take before providing a response.
  3. Selection of the LLM provider, which in this case is OpenAI GPT4o.

 

 To better understand how you can leverage the Project Agent Builder and how it operates in this Use Case, check out the video below!

 

5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

AI Agents are planned to be released as a BTP Service in H1 2025.

You can additionally leverage this BTP service with Joule Studio to extend beyond the core and collaborate between multi-agents for scenarios. Further roadmap innovations will also allow you to extend your AI Agent with SAP Build Process Automation to automate tasks.

Check out the links below for our AI Roadmap in Q1 and Q2 2025.

Q1 2025 Roadmap - New Generative AI Tool Kit with SAP HANA Cloud: SAP Road Map Explorer

Q2 2025 Roadmap for AI Agents for Advanced Business Scenarios:  SAP Road Map Explorer

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 Further information on AI Agents with Joule Studio can be found here.

entry.title: Peering Options for SAP BTP
entry.content:
Customers occasionally inquire about improving connectivity between their on-premise business systems and SAP BTP, as well as between SAP cloud applications. This document provides options and guidance for implementing such a scenario.

Scenario 1: Connection to One Cloud Provider Using Their Peering Product

The simplest scenario is when customers want to extend their bandwidth or reduce internet latency between their (on-premise) business systems and SAP BTP. For this kind of scenario, there is the option to utilize the peering products of the infrastructure provider that underpin SAP BTP (or the SAP cloud application in question). By optimising the connectivity to the infrastructure below SAP BTP or SAP cloud applications, these systems inherit the advantages of this architecture.

For the three major cloud providers that support SAP BTP, the options are as follows

  • AWS: the corresponding AWS product is called Direct Connect: https://repost.aws/knowledge-center/setup-direct-connect-vif
  • Azure:  the corresponding Azure product is called Direct Peering: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/internet-peering/walkthrough-direct-all
  • For Google Cloud, the corresponding product is called Direct Peering, as well: https://cloud.google.com/network-connectivity/docs/direct-peering?hl=en

All three of these products work in a similar fashion. They require a leased line from the on-premise datacenter of the customer to the respective cloud provider. In addition to the physical connectivity, Layer 3 routing should be implemented using the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) to allow for control on both the customer’s and cloud service provider’s sides (see Figure 1).

(Figure 1)

Scenario 2: Connection to One Cloud Provider Using Specialized Peering Providers

Most companies utilize the services of specialized peering providers like Megaport, Equinix, or a similar company.

These companies enable customers to establish a single physical peering connection with the peering provider, which then allows peering with all cloud service providers. This simplifies the setup process by removing the need for individual peering with each cloud provider. In most cases, customers can manage their peerings with cloud service providers through self-service options (see Figure 2).

(Figure 2)

Scenario 3: Connecting to Multiple Cloud Providers

This more complex use-case is the peering of services which are deployed on two or more different cloud service providers. To implement this scenario, the options are to either set up the routing between cloud providers individually on customer premises or use the capabilities of peering providers, similar to option 2.

 

For the first option, the architecture depicted on Figure 1 would be required. However, this would need to be implemented for all required cloud providers. A BGP setup would then be established between routing instances to connect to the cloud service providers and announce the routes between them (See Figure 3 for reference).

(Figure 3)

For the second option, peering providers typically provide a more automated solution, often in the form of a "managed service".

Many providers offer products that enable such a setup, like Megaport’s Cross-Cloud or Equinix’ Fabric Cloud Router (see Figure 4). In these setups, the peering provider announces the routes it receives from one cloud provider to the other. This way, there is an additional hop on the peering provider when connecting from one cloud provider to the other. This approach provides a fully isolated connectivity between two cloud providers and allows full control over the bandwidth and latency imposed by the connection.

 

(Figure 4)

entry.title: Unlock the Power of SAC Custom Widgets: Personalize Your Excel Exports
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Welcome to my very first blog post!

In this post, we’ll dive into how you can elevate your SAP Analytics Cloud experience by harnessing the power of custom widgets to enhance Excel exports.

Many customers express frustration with the limitations of the standard SAC export functionality, and that's where our custom widget comes in.

Whether it’s dynamic template selection, advanced cell formatting, or image integration, this widget provides unmatched flexibility and control over your exports.

Built using JavaScript libraries, this solution goes beyond the basics, enabling you to create fully tailored Excel files that meet your unique reporting requirements.

Let’s explore the features, benefits, and implementation details of this innovative approach to customizing SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Constructor Initialization

  • Attaches a shadow DOM.
  • Clones the template into the shadow DOM.
  • Loads an external script for ExcelJS.

 

 

constructor() {
    super();
    this._shadowRoot = this.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });
    this._shadowRoot.appendChild(tmpl.content.cloneNode(true));
    this._firstConnection = false;
    this.loadScript('https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/exceljs/dist/exceljs.min.js', () => {});
}

 

 

Load External Script

Dynamically loads an external JavaScript library and executes a callback when loading completes.

Note that with this method, you can integrate any JavaScript library of your choice.

 

 

loadScript(src, callback) {
    let script = document.createElement('script');
    script.type = 'text/javascript';
    script.onload = callback;
    script.src=src;
    document.head.appendChild(script);
}

 

 

 

 

Start XLSX Download

Initiates the download process by fetching data and creating the template.

 

 

 

 

async downloadXLSX(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers) {
    var fullData = this.myDataSource;
    this.createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers);
}

 

 

 

 

Extract Data Properties

Extracts specific properties (dimensions and measures) from the data.

 

 

 

 

// Extracts properties (dimensions and measures) from the given data
    getProperties(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let item = data[0]; // Use the first row as an example
      let row = {};

      // Extract dimension values
      if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
          let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
          if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
            row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
          }
        });
      }

      // Extract measure values
      if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
          let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
          if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
            row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
          }
        });
      }

      return row; // Return the extracted row
    }

 

 

 

 

Generate Headers

Creates an array of headers for the XLSX file.

 

 

 

 

getHeaders(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let headers = [];

      // Add dimension headers
      dimensionsToExtract.forEach(dimensionId => {
        headers.push(dimensionId);
      });

      // Add measure headers
      measuresToExtract.forEach(measureId => {
        headers.push(measureId);
      });

      return headers; // Return the header list
    }

 

 

 

 

Format Data

Prepares data rows based on the specified dimensions and measures.

 

 

 

 

  return data.map(item => {
        let row = {};

        // Format dimensions
        if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
            let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
            if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
              row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
            }
          });
        }

        // Format measures
        if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
            let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
            if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
              row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
            }
          });
        }

        return row; // Return the formatted row
      });
    }

 

 

 

 

Set Column Widths

Configures default column widths and wraps text in cells.

 

 

 

 

setColumnWidths(worksheet) {
      const defaultWidth = 20;
      worksheet.columns.forEach(column => {
        column.width = defaultWidth; // Set default column width
        column.eachCell({ includeEmpty: true }, cell => {
          cell.alignment = { wrapText: true }; // Enable text wrapping
        });
      });
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Headers to the Worksheet

Adds headers with specific styles to the worksheet.

 

 

 

 

addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers) {
      const headerFont = { size: 12, bold: true, color: { argb: 'FF000000' } };
      const headerBorder = {
        top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' }
      };

      headers.forEach((title, index) => {
        let cell = worksheet.getCell(currentRow, index + 1);
        cell.value = title; // Set header text
        cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'center', wrapText: true };
        cell.fill = { type: 'pattern', pattern: 'solid', fgColor: { argb: 'FFFFFFFF' } };
        cell.font = headerFont; // Apply font style
        cell.border = headerBorder; // Apply border style
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Total Row

Adds a row to calculate totals for measures and style them.

 

 

 

 

addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, data, measuresToExtract, measureMapping) {
      const mapping = measureMapping.reduce((acc, entry) => {
        const [technicalName, readableName] = entry.split(":");
        acc[technicalName] = readableName;
        return acc;
      }, {});

      let totalRow = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);

      headers.forEach((header, index) => {
        let cell = totalRow.getCell(index + 1);
        const technicalName = Object.keys(mapping).find(key => mapping[key] === header);

        if (technicalName && measuresToExtract.includes(technicalName)) {
          const total = data.reduce((sum, row) => {
            const value = parseFloat(row[technicalName]) || 0;
            return sum + value;
          }, 0);

          cell.value = total; // Set the calculated total
        } else {
          cell.value = '';
        }

        // Style the total row cells
        cell.font = { bold: true };
        cell.alignment = { horizontal: 'center', vertical: 'middle' };
        cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

 

 

 

 

Add Image

Inserts a logo (Base64 image) into the worksheet.

 

 

 

 

// Adds a logo image to the worksheet at a specified location
    addLogo(workbook, worksheet, imageBase64, cellAddress) {
      const imageId = workbook.addImage({
        base64: imageBase64, // Base64-encoded image data
        extension: 'png' // Image file format
      });
      worksheet.addImage(imageId, cellAddress); // Place the image in the specified cells
    }

 

 

 

 

Create and Download the Excel file

Create and writes the workbook to an XLSX file and triggers the browser download.

 

 

 

 

// Creates and downloads the Excel file with data, headers, and styles
    async createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      const rowData = this.formatData(fullData.data, fullData.metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract);

      const workbook = new ExcelJS.Workbook();
      const worksheet = workbook.addWorksheet('Report');

      let currentRow = 1;

      const logo = this.getImage();
      this.addLogo(workbook, worksheet, logo, 'F1:G5');
      currentRow += 6;

      worksheet.views = [{ state: 'frozen', ySplit: currentRow }];

      currentRow = this.addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers);

      rowData.forEach((data) => {
        let row = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);
        row.values = [
          ...dimensionsToExtract.map((key) => data[key]),
          ...measuresToExtract.map((key) => Number(data[key]))
        ];
        row.height = 40;

        row.eachCell((cell) => {
          cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'left', wrapText: true };
          cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                          bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
        });

        currentRow++;
      });

      currentRow = this.addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, rowData, measuresToExtract, measureMapping);

      this.setColumnWidths(worksheet);

      const buffer = await workbook.xlsx.writeBuffer();
      const url = window.URL.createObjectURL(new Blob([buffer]));
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = url;
      a.download = 'Report.xlsx';
      a.click();
      window.URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
    }

 

 

 

 

 

Full Code

Javascript file:

 

 

 

(function () {
  // Create a template element to hold the HTML structure for the custom element
  let tmpl = document.createElement('template');
  tmpl.innerHTML = ``; // The template is empty for now; can be customized later

  // Define a custom HTML element called 'com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport'
  customElements.define('com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport', class CustomWidget extends HTMLElement {

    // The constructor initializes the custom element and its dependencies
    constructor() {
      super();
      // Attach a shadow DOM to encapsulate the element's DOM and styles
      this._shadowRoot = this.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });
      // Append the cloned template content into the shadow DOM
      this._shadowRoot.appendChild(tmpl.content.cloneNode(true));
      // Track whether the element has connected to the DOM for the first time
      this._firstConnection = false;

      // Load the external ExcelJS library asynchronously
      this.loadScript('https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/exceljs/dist/exceljs.min.js', () => {
        // Callback is empty, as no immediate actions are needed after loading the script
      });
    }

    // Method to dynamically load an external script
    loadScript(src, callback) {
      let script = document.createElement('script');
      script.type = 'text/javascript';
      script.onload = callback; // Execute callback when the script is loaded
      script.src=src; // Set the script source URL
      document.head.appendChild(script); // Add the script to the document's <head>
    }

    //Returns the base64 image
    getImage(){
      return "imagebase64..."; // specify here your image in base64
    }

    // Initiates the XLSX file creation and download process
    async downloadXLSX(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      // Get the full dataset (source not shown in this code)
      var fullData = this.myDataSource;

      // Pass the data and metadata to create the Excel template
      this.createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping);
    }

    // Placeholder method executed when the element is added to the DOM
    connectedCallback() { }

    // Placeholder method for redrawing or refreshing the custom widget
    redraw() { }

    // Extracts properties (dimensions and measures) from the given data
    getProperties(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let item = data[0]; // Use the first row as an example
      let row = {};

      // Extract dimension values
      if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
          let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
          if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
            row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
          }
        });
      }

      // Extract measure values
      if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
        Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
          let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
          if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
            row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
          }
        });
      }

      return row; // Return the extracted row
    }

    // Generates a list of headers based on dimensions and measures
    getHeaders(dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      let headers = [];

      // Add dimension headers
      dimensionsToExtract.forEach(dimensionId => {
        headers.push(dimensionId);
      });

      // Add measure headers
      measuresToExtract.forEach(measureId => {
        headers.push(measureId);
      });

      return headers; // Return the header list
    }

    // Formats the data by mapping dimensions and measures for each row
    formatData(data, metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract) {
      return data.map(item => {
        let row = {};

        // Format dimensions
        if (dimensionsToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.dimensions).forEach(key => {
            let dimensionId = metadata.dimensions[key].id;
            if (dimensionsToExtract.includes(dimensionId)) {
              row[dimensionId] = item[key] ? item[key].label : "";
            }
          });
        }

        // Format measures
        if (measuresToExtract.length > 0) {
          Object.keys(metadata.mainStructureMembers).forEach(key => {
            let measureId = metadata.mainStructureMembers[key].id;
            if (measuresToExtract.includes(measureId)) {
              row[measureId] = item[key] ? item[key].formatted : "";
            }
          });
        }

        return row; // Return the formatted row
      });
    }

    // Sets uniform column widths for the Excel worksheet
    setColumnWidths(worksheet) {
      const defaultWidth = 20;
      worksheet.columns.forEach(column => {
        column.width = defaultWidth; // Set default column width
        column.eachCell({ includeEmpty: true }, cell => {
          cell.alignment = { wrapText: true }; // Enable text wrapping
        });
      });
    }

    // Adds headers to the worksheet with styles applied
    addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers) {
      const headerFont = { size: 12, bold: true, color: { argb: 'FF000000' } };
      const headerBorder = {
        top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' }
      };

      headers.forEach((title, index) => {
        let cell = worksheet.getCell(currentRow, index + 1);
        cell.value = title; // Set header text
        cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'center', wrapText: true };
        cell.fill = { type: 'pattern', pattern: 'solid', fgColor: { argb: 'FFFFFFFF' } };
        cell.font = headerFont; // Apply font style
        cell.border = headerBorder; // Apply border style
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

    // Adds a total row to calculate and display measure totals
    addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, data, measuresToExtract, measureMapping) {
      const mapping = measureMapping.reduce((acc, entry) => {
        const [technicalName, readableName] = entry.split(":");
        acc[technicalName] = readableName;
        return acc;
      }, {});

      let totalRow = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);

      headers.forEach((header, index) => {
        let cell = totalRow.getCell(index + 1);
        const technicalName = Object.keys(mapping).find(key => mapping[key] === header);

        if (technicalName && measuresToExtract.includes(technicalName)) {
          const total = data.reduce((sum, row) => {
            const value = parseFloat(row[technicalName]) || 0;
            return sum + value;
          }, 0);

          cell.value = total; // Set the calculated total
        } else {
          cell.value = '';
        }

        // Style the total row cells
        cell.font = { bold: true };
        cell.alignment = { horizontal: 'center', vertical: 'middle' };
        cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                        bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
      });

      return currentRow + 1; // Move to the next row
    }

    // Adds a logo image to the worksheet at a specified location
    addLogo(workbook, worksheet, imageBase64, cellAddress) {
      const imageId = workbook.addImage({
        base64: imageBase64, // Base64-encoded image data
        extension: 'png' // Image file format
      });
      worksheet.addImage(imageId, cellAddress); // Place the image in the specified cells
    }

    // Creates and downloads the Excel file with data, headers, and styles
    async createTemplate(fullData, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract, headers, measureMapping) {
      const rowData = this.formatData(fullData.data, fullData.metadata, dimensionsToExtract, measuresToExtract);

      const workbook = new ExcelJS.Workbook();
      const worksheet = workbook.addWorksheet('Report');

      let currentRow = 1;

      const logo = this.getImage();
      this.addLogo(workbook, worksheet, logo, 'F1:G5');
      currentRow += 6;

      worksheet.views = [{ state: 'frozen', ySplit: currentRow }];

      currentRow = this.addHeaders(worksheet, currentRow, headers);

      rowData.forEach((data) => {
        let row = worksheet.getRow(currentRow);
        row.values = [
          ...dimensionsToExtract.map((key) => data[key]),
          ...measuresToExtract.map((key) => Number(data[key]))
        ];
        row.height = 40;

        row.eachCell((cell) => {
          cell.alignment = { vertical: 'middle', horizontal: 'left', wrapText: true };
          cell.border = { top: { style: 'thin' }, left: { style: 'thin' },
                          bottom: { style: 'thin' }, right: { style: 'thin' } };
        });

        currentRow++;
      });

      currentRow = this.addTotalRow(worksheet, currentRow, headers, rowData, measuresToExtract, measureMapping);

      this.setColumnWidths(worksheet);

      const buffer = await workbook.xlsx.writeBuffer();
      const url = window.URL.createObjectURL(new Blob([buffer]));
      const a = document.createElement('a');
      a.href = url;
      a.download = 'Report.xlsx';
      a.click();
      window.URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
    }
  });
})();

 

 

 

JSON file:

 

 

 

{
    "name": "Custom Widget Export Excel Universal",
    "description": "Custom Widget Export Excelc Universal",
    "eula": "EULA",
    "vendor": "SAP",
    "license": "",
    "id": "com.sap.sac.customwidgetexcelexport",
    "newInstancePrefix": "customwidgetexcelexport",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "icon": "",
    "webcomponents": [
        {
            "kind": "main",
            "tag": "com-sap-sac-customwidgetexcelexport",
            "url": "/stdExport.js",
            "integrity": "",
            "ignoreIntegrity": true
        }
    ],
    "properties": {},
    "dataBindings": {
        "myDataSource": {
            "feeds": [
                {
                    "id": "dimensions",
                    "description": "Dimensions",
                    "type": "dimension"
                },
                {
                    "id": "measures",
                    "description": "Measures",
                    "type": "mainStructureMember"
                }
            ]
        }
    },
    "methods": {
        "downloadXLSX": {
            "returnType": "",
            "description": "",
            "parameters": [
                {"name": "dimensionsToExtract", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "measuresToExtract", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "headers", "type": "string[]"},
                {"name": "measureMapping", "type": "string[]"}
            ]
        }
    },
    "events": {}
}

 

 

 

 

Sample of usage

Add the custom widget to your story. Since the widget lacks a graphical component, it will appear as a transparent object.

Next, as the custom widget uses Data Bindings, attach the model from which you want to fetch data, and specify the measures and dimensions to include in the dataset.

Attach below script to an event (a button click or others) to trigger the export:

 

 

 

var dim = ["Version","Item","Date","Person"]; //Technical names
var measures = ["[Account].[parentId].&[Quantity]","[Account].[parentId].&[Price]"]; //Technical names
var headers = ["Category","Item","Date","Person","Quantity","Price"]; //Headers you want to show as table header in the Excel file
var measureMapping = [
  "[Account].[parentId].&[Quantity]:Quantity",
  "[Account].[parentId].&[Price]:Price"
]; //mapping between the measure technical name and the name used as table header
customwidgetexcelexport_1.downloadXLSX(dim,measures,headers,measureMapping);

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

In conclusion, this custom widget offers a flexible and powerful way to enhance your SAP Analytics Cloud Excel exports. While this is not a final version, it serves as a foundation you can build upon to meet your specific needs. Whether it’s adding colors, images, or hyperlinks, you have the freedom to design your Excel templates exactly as you envision. The possibilities for customization are virtually limitless, allowing you to create exports that align perfectly with your reporting requirements.

See you in the next blog! 😊
